P.o. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET tij\,J,iG, ~ACRAMENTO, 95814 
Mr. Dennis Ornoto 
Deputy City Attorney 
1685 Main Street 
Santa Monica, California 90401 
Dear Mr. Omoto: 
February 11, 1977 
This letter is to rescind advice given to Richard Goldman of your 
office by letter dated July 29, 1975. 
The letter stated that Santa Monica's campaign disclosure ordinance 
could require the filing of post-election campaign statements 
twenty-one days after elections rather than sixty-five days as 
required in Government Code Section 84200. 
However, on July 6, 
1976, The Fair Political Practices Commission adopted 2 FPPC 
ops. 91, (request number 75-125 fuom Edwin L. Miller, District 
Attorney, 
County of San Diego) a copy of which is enclosed, which 
concludes that a local government may not enact an ordinance which differs 
from and supersedes the campaign disclosure provisions of the Political 
Reform Act. 
We advise, therefore, that the Santa Monica City 
ordinance be amended to comply with the standards set forth in the 
opinion to make its post-election filing deadline consistent with that 
set forth in Government Code Section 84200. 
If you have any questions, please contact me. 
Very truly yours, 
Nan Emmerson 
Technical Assistant 
TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (9161322.5660 • 
EXECUTIVE/LEGAl

sections.question_synthetic: Can a local government ordinance require campaign disclosure filing deadlines that differ from those mandated by the California Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: No. The FPPC rescinded prior advice and concluded that local governments cannot enact ordinances that differ from or supersede the campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act. Santa Monica's ordinance requiring post-election statements twenty-one days after elections must be amended to comply with Government Code Section 84200's sixty-five day requirement.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a rescission letter with no traditional QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections. The legal question and FPPC's position are embedded in the body text. Document references 2 FPPC ops. 91 (request 75-125) as the basis for the rescission. Dated February 11, 1977.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Can a local government ordinance require campaign disclosure filing deadlines that differ from those mandated by the California Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: No. The FPPC rescinded prior advice and concluded that local governments cannot enact ordinances that differ from or supersede the campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act. Santa Monica's ordinance requiring post-election statements twenty-one days after elections must be amended to comply with Government Code Section 84200's sixty-five day requirement.

The letter stated that Santa Monica's campaign disclosure ordinance could require the filing of post-election campaign statements twenty-one days after elections rather than sixty-five days as required in Government Code Section 84200. However, on July 6, 1976, The Fair Political Practices Commission adopted 2 FPPC ops. 91, (request number 75-125 fuom Edwin L. Miller, District Attorney, County of San Diego) a copy of which is enclosed, which concludes that a local government may not enact an ordinance which differs from and supersedes the campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act. We advise, therefore, that the Santa Monica City ordinance be amended to comply with the standards set forth in the opinion to make its post-election filing deadline consistent with that set forth in Government Code Section 84200. embedding.summary: FPPC rescinds a 1975 advice letter and clarifies that local governments cannot impose campaign disclosure requirements that supersede state law, requiring Santa Monica to amend its ordinance to comply with Government Code Section 84200.
